properties.name: Riverview park
properties.description: Riverview Park was an amusement park in the Roscoe Village neighborhood of Chicago, which opened in 1904. At the time, it housed the largest downhill roller coaster in the world.

properties.name: The Hyde Park Hotel
properties.description: Helped establish the neighborhood as affluent.

properties.name: Village Center Mall
properties.description: A strip mall that housed a supermarket, the Original Pancake House, drug store, and liquor store.

properties.name: Chicago Stadium
properties.replacement: parking lot for the United Center
properties.description: The largest indoor arena in the world at the time it was built. It was the home of the NHL Chicago Blackhawks and the NBA’s Chicago Bulls and hosted five United States presidential nominating conventions.

properties.name: Mecca Flats
properties.description: Originally constructed as a hotel for the World's Columbian Exposition of 1893, the 96-unit Mecca building became an apartment building after the fair. The building was acquired by Illinois Tech around 1941, and was demolished in 1952 to make way for the construction of S.R. Crown Hall.

properties.name: Gerri’s Palm Tavern
properties.replacement: Gallery Guichard Great Migration Sculpture Garden
properties.description: The tavern was once a center of Black life and culture for decades.

properties.name: Prentice Women’s Hospital
properties.replacement: Louis A. Simpson and Kimberly K. Querrey Biomedical Research Center
properties.description: Iconic clover-shaped structure that was one of the first major buildings designed using early computer-aided technology.

properties.name: Kraft building
properties.description: The Kraft building housed both the company’s headquarters, food laboratories and a food-processing plant and was the largest building erected downtown in 1937-38. Preservationists lamented the loss of the 1939 building that saw the birth of Parkay margarine.

properties.name: The Arcade
properties.description: One of the social and economic cornerstones of Pullman, the industrial company town, which contained a shopping mall, an auditorum, a post office, and a community center all in one.

properties.name: United States Custom House, Court House, and Post Office
properties.description: This federal building housed the United States Custom House, Court House, and Post Office. Due to poor planning and construction, by the 1890s was considered dangerous and too small. It was notoriously overcrowded, poorly planned, and suffered from structural issues, including failing plaster and plumbing.

properties.name: Chicago Federal Building
properties.description: The Chicago Federal Building was constructed between 1898 and 1905 in the Beaux Arts style by architect Henry Ives Cobb. It housed the Midwest's federal courts, main post office, and other government bureaus

properties.name: Tacoma Building
properties.description: Tacoma Building was an early skyscraper and the first to use a complete iron and steel skeleton frame.

properties.name: Masonic Temple
properties.description: The Masonic Temple, with 21 stories, was the tallest building in the city until the 1920s.

properties.name: Olson Park and Waterfall
properties.description: During the opening of the park in 1935, the land was symbolically deeded to the Native American, in recognition of the 100th anniversary of the expulsion after the Blackhawk war. The Chicago Tribune named Olson Park as the first of "Chicago's Seven Lost Wonders".

properties.name: Great Central Station
properties.description: Great Central Station, also known as Great Central Depot, was an intercity train station in Chicago, Illinois, owned by the Illinois Central Railroad (IC). It opened in 1856 and for a time was the largest building in downtown Chicago.

properties.name: Zum Deutschen Eck
properties.description: The restaurant was a premier cultural institution for German-Americans.

properties.name: The Schiller Theater Building
properties.description: Serving as a cultural hub for German-American opera, theater, and social causes, news of its planned demolition sparked Chicago's modern architectural preservation movement.

properties.name: Lakeside Building
properties.description: A prominent post-Great Fire structure that served as a key commercial hub in the city's reconstruction efforts.

properties.name: The Lake Shore Drive ‘S’ Curve Bottleneck
properties.description: The road featured two sharp 90-degree turns, fitting for the slower vehicles of the time. However, as traffic speed increased, this once necessary design gradually became a source of congestion and safety concerns. Then the city redesigned the road to become what is today the lake shore drive

properties.name: Louis Henry Sullivan House
properties.description: Known as the “prophet of modern architecture,” Louis Sullivan advocated creating buildings that honestly mirrored their time, place and technology. Damaged by fire and abandoned, the house was demolished in 1970

properties.name: The 1885 Cook County Courthouse and City Hall
properties.description: The City Hall was built in the French Renaissance style as a dual structure for joint occupancy by city and county. However, as soon as the building was finished, various problems emerged, which forced the decision for a new city hall and a new country building. Structural failurues were caused by the money being used in bribery and election expenses. The building revealed the most corrupt period of politics in Cook County

properties.name: The Historical Haven School
properties.description: The Haven School included modern amenities for its era, such as steam heating, thorough ventilation, and a gymnasium on the fourth floor, a novel feature in school design at the time. Its design and construction set a high standard for future school buildings in Chicago, influencing the development of public education facilities in the city.

properties.name: Marshall Field House
properties.description: Designed by Richard Morris Hunt, the architect behind iconic structures like the Vanderbilt’s The Breakers in Newport, Rhode Island, the mansion was a masterpiece of 19th-century architecture.

properties.name: The William Borden House
properties.description: Designed by Robert Morris Hunt, the mansion was a three-story, French Renaissance-style chateau with imported stone carvings from France, reflecting the opulence and architectural ambition of Chicago’s elite.The demolition of the house is often noted by architectural historians as a major loss of a Gilded Age masterpiece, though its interior was extensively photographed by Richard Nickel before its destruction.

properties.name: Swift & Company Meatpacking Warehouse
properties.replacement: part of Stockyards Industrial Park and also vacant land
properties.description: Swift's headquarters was a major center for the nation's beef supply. In the 1920s, the area where that swift headquarters occupied was Chicago's meatpacking district. Gustavus Franklin Swift founded the Midwestern meatpacking empire and helped develop the first ice-cooled railroad car. Without him, Chicago might not have become the epicenter of the industry a century ago.

properties.name: Italian Court Building
properties.description: The Italian court was a 20th-century mixed-use building in the state of Illinois in the United States and served as a gathering spot for Chicago's bohemian artists and the venue for poetry readings.

properties.name: Chicago Machinery Exchange
properties.description: The building was constructed during a period when Chicago was rapidly industrializing, and it served as a warehouse for the Chicago Machinery Exchange, a key player in the city’s industrial and commercial growth.

properties.name: Ida B. Wells Homes
properties.description: The Ida B. Wells Homes, which also included the Clarence Darrow Homes and Madden Park Homes, was a Chicago Housing Authority (CHA) public housing project

properties.name: W.J. Cassidy Tire Company
properties.description: Cassidy Tire Company is a historic, family-owned business that has played a vital role in Chicago’s automotive, architectural, and economic history for over a century.

properties.name: Doctors Hospital of Hyde Park
properties.description: It first opened as the Illinois Central Community Hospital serving railroad employees before becoming the Doctors' Hospital of Hyde Park when it closed in 2000.

properties.name: Mickelberry Sausage Company plant
properties.description: The explosion at the Mickelberry Sausage Company plantone of the worst tragedies in Chicago history. Nine people were killed, including four firefighters. Seventy people were injured.

properties.name: Phenix Building
properties.description: After its completion, it was considered one of Chicago's most magnificent architectural structures.

properties.name: Cooley Vocational High School
properties.description: The school’s student body underwent a demographic shift in the 1960s. Initially, it was 69% White and 31% African-American in 1958, but by 1967, 98% of the student population was African-American, reflecting the broader racial and social changes in Chicago during the era.

properties.name: Regal Theater
properties.description: The venue featured performances by some of the most celebrated African-American entertainers such as: Nat King Cole, Louis Armstrong, Ella Fitzgerald, Miles Davis, Sammy Davis Jr., Duke Ellington, The Temptations, The Supremes, Stevie Wonder, Etta James, Dionne Warwick and James Brown.

properties.name: McCarthy Building
properties.replacement: 22 West Washington Block 37 retail and office complex
properties.description: A major case in Chicago history where one of the oldest post-Great Fire structures and designated landmark was demolished for the development of Block 37.

properties.name: South Side Masonic Temple
properties.description: The building was a historic 1921 Classical Revival building known for its Egyptian Revival, Moorish, and Art Deco elements and served as a community hub until being abandoned in the 1950s.

properties.name: Paradise Theatre
properties.description: The paradise theater was billed as the world’s most beautiful theater. In 1956, owners Balaban and Katz closed the theater, demolished the building and sold the land to be developed as a supermarket

properties.name: Savoy Ballroom
properties.description: Originally featuring primarily Jazz artists, including Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, Duke Ellington, Earl Hines, Stan Kenton, Dizzy Gillespie, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Gene Krupa, Woody Herman, the Savoy Ballroom was the largest dancehall in the South side community

properties.name: E2 Nightclub
properties.description: The site of a deadly stampede in 2003 which led to 21 deaths and more than 50 injuries.

properties.name: Ravenswood Presbyterian Church
properties.description: The church was a place of shared memories and traditions for generations of Ravenswood residents. Since the congregation left the building and moved to Mayfair in fall 2024, it was sold and reports claimed that the building is planned to be demolished.

properties.name: Old University of Chicago
properties.description: The Old University of Chicago was founded by Baptist Church leaders. The school was constantly plagued by financial difficulties and was forced to close in 1886. While the present-day University of Chicago, which was established in 1891, is a separate legal entity and in a different location, it recognized Old University of Chicago alumni as its own and maintained a number of other continuities from its pre-1890 origins

properties.name: Academy of Music
properties.description: The third Academy of Music known also known as Shelby's Academy operated as a theatre, then converted to a silent films movie theatre. Its eventual demolition in 1936 marked the end of an era for Chicago’s early theatrical history

properties.name: Michael Reese Hospital
properties.description: Portions of the hospital campus were over 100 years old and believed by some preservationists to hold historic value. The older buildings were constructed in an ornate style.

properties.name: Dexter Building
properties.description: It was known for its structural innovation and considered as crucial to Sullivan's architectural style, a precursor to the renowned Auditorium Building.

properties.name: Crawford Generating Station
properties.description: One of the city’s key coal-burning generating stations that became a major pollution site whose closure was tied to policy change.

properties.name: Stateway Gardens
properties.description: A major public housing complex known for extreme racial segregation and high crime rates

properties.name: Montauk Building
properties.description: A 10-story office building that was considered by many historians as the first tall commercial building in Chicago built from masonry construction.

properties.name: Midway Gardens
properties.description: The Midway Gardens was a 360,000 square feet indoor and outdoor entertainment center that was known for its beer garden and sculptures. The design was so well-built that the demolition company went bankrupt after the demolition work.

properties.name: Home Insurance Building
properties.description: The Home insurance company was widely considered the first skyscraper in the world.

properties.name: Brach's Candy Factory
properties.description: Brach's candy company was once one of the country's largest confectionary plants

properties.name: Belmont Rocks
properties.description: This was considered a sanctuary for the LGBTQ+ community especially during the AIDS crisis.

properties.name: Chicago Stock Exchange
properties.description: This building was considered a masterpiece of commercial architecture and was designed by Adler & Sullivan.

properties.name: Jane Addams Homes
properties.description: This was one of the first three public housing projects in Chicago.

properties.name: Dexter Park
properties.description: The horse racing track was also the first home of the White Stockings baseball team and hosted other events like wrestling.

properties.name: International Amphitheatre
properties.description: This was known as the site of four Democratic National Conventions and two Republican National Conventions. It also served as an entertainment venue that hosted Elvis, the Beatles, and the Bulls.

properties.name: Hull House
properties.description: Hull House became a leading model for social reform in the United States. It introduced services like childcare, public playgrounds, and educational classes for immigrant communities. They were also active in the reforms of child labor laws, women's rights, and education.

properties.name: Truc Lam Buddhist Temple
properties.description: Became a case of failed landmark protection and the demolition also served as a sign of gentrification in the neighborhood.

properties.name: Fort Dearborn
properties.description: A rebuilding of the original U.S. Army fort that was burned down during the 1812 Battle of Fort Dearborn. Most of the structure was destroyed in a fire or torn down to make way for the city's expansion.

properties.name: Lexington Hotel
properties.description: Known as Al Capone's headquarter from 1928 until his arrest. Eventually, it turned into a 400-room brothel.

properties.name: Central Music Hall
properties.description: Widely considered as Adler's first important building, and served as a vital cultural engine in the Loop, bridging commerce with the arts.

properties.name: Chicago Central Station
properties.description: The station was a gateway to Chicago for African Americans who settled in nearby neighborhoods such as Bronzeville.

properties.name: Archer Daniels Midland Wheat Mill
properties.replacement: In development for a mixed-use project
properties.description: Largest flour mill in the state when it was first developed.

properties.name: Chicago and Northwestern Passenger Terminal
properties.description: This terminal was known for not only its distinctive clock tower, but also for setting new standards for passenger care.

properties.name: Meigs Field
properties.description: An iconic, single-runway airport that was demolished overnight without prior notice.

properties.name: Comiskey Park
properties.description: This was a stadium that once served as home for the White Sox, Caridinals, Chicago American Giants, and the Chicago Sting.

properties.name: Chicago Grand Central Station
properties.description: This was considered an architectural marvel and engineering feat. It was also one of the few stations that featured an integrated hotel.

properties.name: The Morrison Hotel
properties.description: This was once the world's tallest hotel for 30 years and remained the tallest hotel in Chicago up until it was demolished.

properties.name: Sauganash Hotel
properties.description: Chicago's first hotel and first theater.

properties.name: Wigwam
properties.description: This location served as the site for the 1860 Republican National Convention where Abraham Lincoln became the party's nominee for the presidential election.

properties.name: Former Chicago Sun Times Building
properties.description: Known to be an eyesore and served as the headquarters to Chicago Sun Times Newspaper.

properties.name: Henrici's
properties.description: This location was actually the second after the original was destroyed in the Great Fire. The restaurant was known as "Chicago's Most Famous Restaurant.

properties.name: Granada Theatre
properties.description: This was once a popular rock-concert venue and movie theatre. At the time, it was one of the three largest theatres in the city.

properties.name: A.J. Stone Building
properties.description: This was dubbed the first skyscraper on the West Side.

properties.name: Iroquois Theatre
properties.description: The building had a fire that killed 600 people just a month after it opened.

properties.name: Robert Taylor Homes
properties.description: This was the largest public housing project in United States history prior to its demolition.

properties.name: SMC Cartage Co. garage
properties.description: This was the site of the infamous St. Valentine’s Day Massacre.

properties.name: Cabrini-Green Homes
properties.description: Considered a national symbol of the failure of high-rise public housing policies.

properties.name: Palmer Mansion
properties.description: At one point, this was the largest private residence in the city. The construction of the mansion essentially led to the establishment of the Gold Coast neighborhood.

properties.name: White City Amusement Park
properties.description: A premier Chicago entertainment hub that was promoted as the largest of its kind in the US. It also hosted the first Goodyear Blimp assembly.

properties.name: Garrick Theater
properties.description: This theater had one of the most publicized preservation campaigns in the city's history.

properties.name: Edgewater Beach Hotel
properties.description: The hotel was known for its notable guests, including Martin Luther King Jr..

properties.name: Mode Theatre
properties.description: This was a notable neighborhood cinema.

properties.name: Grand Pacific Hotel
properties.description: One of two prominent hotels built in Chicago after the Great Chicago Fire.

properties.name: Union Stock Yards
properties.description: Chicago’s first hog packinghouses that fundamentally transformed American agriculture, industrial production, consumer habits, and labor practices.

properties.name: Vendome Theater
properties.description: This theater catered to black audiences who were denied admission to 'whites-only' venues in their neighborhood.

properties.name: The Pullman Building
properties.description: This was the headquarter of George Pullman's railroad car empire.

properties.name: Pilgrim Baptist Church
properties.replacement: Planned: National Museum of Gospel Music
properties.description: Consicered as the birthplace of gospel music.

properties.name: Chicago Coliseum
properties.description: This was a major political and cultural hub that also served as a rock concert venue, an arena for hosting the first-ever roller derby event and early interracial basketball tournaments, as well as ice hockey.

properties.name: Pekin Theatre
properties.description: This was the first Black-owned musical and vaudeville theater in the United States.

properties.name: Grand Avenue Radar Track
properties.description: The site was used for testing mobile radar units during World War II.

properties.name: Damen Silos
properties.description: A massive concrete grain elevator complex that was later used for film backdrops.

properties.name: Little Jim’s Tavern
properties.description: This was Boystown's first-established and longest-running gay bar.

properties.name: Man's Country/Chicago
properties.description: Chicago's longest-running gay bathhouse that hosted celebrity performances and served as a concert venue, dance club, community space, gym, and sex club.

properties.name: Hyde Park House
properties.description: Notable hotel that Mary Todd Lincoln and the Prince of Wales stayed at during their time in Chicago.

properties.name: Maxwell Street Market
properties.description: A crucial cultural hub for immigrant and Black communities, and is considered the birthplace of Chicago Blues. It was at one point considered the world’s largest open-air market

properties.name: Mahlon D. Ogden Mansion
properties.description: This was known for being the only house in the path of the Great Fire of 1871 which was not burned.

properties.name: Temperance Temple
properties.description: A significant intersection of female activism, philanthropy, and corporate ambition in late 19th-century America happened in this building.

properties.name: The Relic House
properties.description: This was a symbol of the city's resilience and served as a popular tourist attraction. It eventually transitioned into a saloon, German restaurant, and even a speakeasy during the Prohibition era. It was also a known gathering spot for artists.

properties.name: Rainbo Arena
properties.description: Multipurpose building with a long history of occupancies: outdoor gardens that was transformed into an indoor sports arena, having a training rink for several Olympic figure skaters that was also used as the Blackhawk's practice facility, and even housing a pro bowling alley. Afterwards, when it became the Kinetic Playground up until its demolition, the location was known as the driving force in the music business for hosting famous rock bands and musicians.

properties.name: McCormick Reaper Works II
properties.description: At its peak, the complex produced the famous McCormick reaper, which revolutionized farming, along with other agricultural machinery and even military equipment.
